title: Grief and loss: how to mourn instead of "holding it together" at any cost
preview: A piece about men’s grief without drama or shame: how to live through the loss of someone close without being left alone with that pain.
content:
Losing someone close is a blow that changes your world for good. However much life seems to prepare us, the moment someone is simply gone still knocks the ground out from under you. Grief is a natural response to loss, not a sign of weakness or "not being put together enough." But men are often offered a different script: "stay strong", "be there for everyone else", "don’t show how much it hurts." Inside everything may be falling apart, while on the outside you’re expected to be the calm, solid one who "handles" even this.

In practice it can look like this: you keep yourself busy, sort out arrangements, support other people, deal with the funeral, paperwork, everyday logistics. People tell you how well you’re coping and how "strong" you are. At night, though, flashes of memory hit, your chest feels empty, there’s a lump in your throat, and thoughts circle around "how did this happen" and everything you think you did "wrong" in the relationship. Sometimes a wave of anger comes – at doctors, family, yourself, the person who died, God, the world. Sometimes you feel nothing and think, "what’s wrong with me, why am I not crying like everyone else?"

On top of that come the usual messages: "men don’t cry", "I have to be the rock, not fall apart", "if I let myself break, who will take care of everything?" Because of this, grief often gets pushed deeper down. What shows on the surface is irritability, shutting people out, snapping over small things, and burying yourself in even more work so you don’t have to sit alone with what’s inside. But pretending it doesn’t hurt doesn’t make the loss itself any smaller. It just leaves it without care or support.

It matters to recognize that you have the right to grieve in your own way. Not according to textbook stages, not according to what others expect, not "the right way," but in the way that fits your experience. Some days that might mean feeling a heavy weight in your chest and not being able to get out of bed. Other days there might be some sense of order and even moments of laughter that you later feel guilty about. Grief doesn’t move in a straight line, and that’s normal. What isn’t healthy is banning yourself from feeling anything just because you’re a man.

Taking care of yourself during this time doesn’t mean abandoning others or forgetting the person you lost. It looks like basic things: managing to eat something, getting at least a little sleep, stepping outside sometimes, not numbing everything with alcohol, not burying yourself in endless work. Sometimes it’s one or two honest sentences to a friend or partner instead of the standard "I’m fine": "this really hurts", "I honestly don’t know how to live with this yet." Sometimes it’s having a place where you don’t have to be strong for everyone, like a therapist’s office.

In the app there is a theme dedicated to grief and loss. The cards there won’t tell you the "right" way to mourn – there isn’t one. They help you notice what is actually going on with you: which emotions you don’t allow yourself, where you’re stuck between anger, guilt, and emptiness, how you might keep someone’s memory alive without living every day as if it’s the day they died. These are small steps that don’t "fix" the loss, but give it a place in your life so that, over time, you can live again instead of just getting through the days.

You can open one grief card and simply see how it resonates. Try one exercise: write down what was left unsaid, remember one warm moment without forcing yourself to "move on", do one small thing in the person’s memory. The grief won’t disappear, but it may feel a little less lonely. A man who admits that he’s in pain and gives himself permission to feel it is not betraying his strength or the person he lost. He is giving himself a chance to come through this as a living human being instead of turning into stone. Opening the grief theme in the app is one way to start walking that path without doing it completely alone.
